text.title: Pixar Framework, Story Elements, and Data Storytelling
text.summary: The Pixar causal story structure for change narratives, hooks and concrete language for opening impact, and the before/after/so-what pattern for data storytelling.

section.title: Pixar Framework

rule.statement: Use the Pixar causal chain for change stories and case studies: each "because of that" link must be a direct consequence of the prior event, not a separate observation.
rule.rationale: The "because of that" connector forces the narrator to establish causality, not just sequence. Without causality, change stories read as coincidences rather than evidence of impact.

example.note: Pixar structure applied to a business automation win.
example.code: Once upon a time: our team spent 10 hours/week on manual reporting. Every day: they copy-pasted data between 5 spreadsheets. One day: we implemented automated dashboards. Because of that: reporting time dropped to 1 hour. Because of that: the team focused on analysis, not data entry. Until finally: we identified $500K in cost savings.

section.reference: Case study template using Pixar causal structure
4-section presentation outline: Hook / Problem / Solution / CTA

rule.statement: Open every presentation or document with a hook in the first 30 seconds: use a surprising statistic, a bold contradiction, or a specific story — not an agenda slide.
rule.rationale: Audiences form engagement decisions in the first 30 seconds. An agenda slide answers a question nobody is asking; a hook creates a question the audience wants answered.

list.item: Surprising stat: "90% of startups fail. Here's why we won't."
Question: "What if you could deploy without fear?"
Bold statement: "Meetings are broken. Let me prove it."
Story: "Last Tuesday at 2am, our server crashed..."
Contradiction: "The best code is no code."

rule.statement: Replace abstract outcomes with concrete, numbered results. Apply the "so what?" test to every data point: if the implication is not explicit, state it.
rule.rationale: Abstract language ("improved efficiency") is forgettable because it activates no mental image. Concrete numbers anchor understanding and credibility.

example.code: "Cut processing time from 4 hours to 15 minutes" "Reduced support tickets by 60%" "Blocked 10,000 attacks last month"

example.note: The so-what escalation for a reliability metric.
example.code: Bad: "We have 99.9% uptime" Better: "99.9% uptime means only 8.7 hours of downtime per year" Best: "99.9% uptime means your customers never see an error page"

antipattern.description: Burying the main recommendation at the end after extensive background.
antipattern.reason: Decision-makers who stop reading mid-document never receive the recommendation. Lead with the answer per Pyramid Principle.

antipattern.description: Ending a presentation without a specific call to action.
antipattern.reason: Vague endings produce "let me think about it" deferrals. A clear ask defines what decision is being requested and when.
